Title: MoF unveils support measures for MSMEs amid global energy crisis
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Ministry of Finance (MoF) today announced a series of measures aimed at supporting micro, small and medium-sized enterprises (MSMEs) affected by the global energy crisis.
The initiatives include a RM5 billion allocation under the Syarikat Jaminan Pembiayaan Perniagaan (SJPP), an extended transition period for e-invoicing implementation and interim exemptions on import duties and sales tax for re-imported Malaysian goods.
In a statement, MoF said the RM5 billion allocation will provide financing guarantees of up to 80 per cent (higher than the usual 70 per cent), with an extended guarantee period of up to 10 years.
"SJPP will also collaborate with banks to facilitate loan restructuring and rescheduling, as well as targeted repayment assistance," it said.
Meanwhile, the MoF said the government is also extending the transition period for e-invoicing implementation.
It noted that businesses with annual sales between RM1 million and RM5 million will be given an additional 12 months until Dec 31, 2027, to comply, with flexibility to issue consolidated e-invoices without penalties during the period.
In addition, the government is considering interim exemptions on import duties and sales tax for reimported Malaysian goods that could not complete the export process due to disruptions linked to ongoing geopolitical conflicts.
The government will continue to monitor and assess the need to facilitate tax treatment for businesses affected by the West Asian conflict, it said.
Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said this initiative forms part of the government's ongoing efforts since the onset of the crisis to ensure that every measure implemented truly reaches those in need.
Anwar, who is also the finance minister, said the government will continue to strengthen collaboration with financial institutions, industry players and entrepreneurs to ensure that every measure taken helps sustain business operations and protect jobs.
"This is not a temporary situation; we must be prepared for gradual and long-term changes," he added.
In line with the Madani economic framework, the government remains committed to strengthening national economic resilience amid global uncertainties while ensuring that assistance is delivered in a targeted manner to those most in need.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'We were shocked,' says father of man shot by suspected cattle thieves
Article:
TANAH MERAH: The family of a man shot in the face by suspected cattle smugglers in Felda Kemahang 1 early today is still reeling from the incident.
Abdul Kadir Mohamad, 73, said he was at home with his 64-year-old wife, Siti Zabedah Besar and their son Amirul Akil when they received news about the attack involving his eldest son, Mohamad Khairi Amri, 42.
The former Felda settler said a friend called about 7am informing them that Khairi had been shot and was being treated at Tanah Merah Hospital.
"A friend called and told us to go there… he said my son had been shot and was in hospital. We were shocked.
"I have yet to see him, but I was told he was hit in the cheek and chin," he said when met at his home in Kampung Kemahang 1 here.
The father of 13 said Khairi has been working as a caretaker for villagers' cattle for the past seven or eight years, tending to about 20 cows.
Kadir said his son usually looks after the livestock at a cowshed in Lalang Pepuyu, about 10km from their house.
"He has been taking care of the villagers' cattle for years. That is his routine," he said.
Amirul Akil said some 40 villagers rushed to help his brother after hearing the commotion.
"The villagers said they saw five or six people arrive on motorcycles and tried to steal the cows.
"The group, believed to be from a neighbouring country, escaped later," he added.
Amirul Akil said his brother is in stable condition in hospital.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Antarctic ozone healing, but climate change poses new threat
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A Malaysian-led scientific study has confirmed that the ozone layer above Antarctica is gradually recovering, but warns that climate change is introducing new risks that could affect its long-term stability.
The research, led by Justin Sentian of Universiti Malaysia Sabah, was published this year in the journal Atmospheric Research.
Analysing 19 years of Nasa satellite data, the international research team found that ozone levels over Antarctica are increasing at a measurable rate – offering fresh evidence that global efforts under the Montreal Protocol are working.
However, the study found that recovery is uneven throughout the year.
"As sunlight returns to Antarctica in late winter, it triggers chemical reactions that destroy ozone.
"While winter shows strong ozone concentration, the spring phase sees a decline, with the upward trend weakening significantly. This narrow window remains when the atmosphere is most vulnerable to ultraviolet (UV) penetration," the study noted.
The ozone layer acts as Earth's natural shield against harmful UV radiation. Without it, higher levels of UV rays would reach the surface, increasing risks such as skin cancer, eye damage and weakened immune systems, while also affecting crops, marine life and ecosystems.
Beyond seasonal variation, researchers identified a new factor influencing recovery – changes in atmospheric circulation linked to global warming.
The study found that a strengthening of the Brewer-Dobson Circulation is transporting more water vapour into the polar stratosphere, potentially accelerating chemical processes that contribute to ozone depletion.
Despite these emerging challenges, the study concludes that the reduction of ozone-depleting substances — particularly chlorine and bromine — remains the dominant driver of recovery.
The ozone hole, first identified in the 1980s, became a defining symbol of environmental damage caused by human activity. Malaysia ratified the Montreal Protocol in 1989 as part of the global response.
Sentian, who heads the Climate Change Research Lab and Polar Research Group at UMS, was also recognised in 2023 as the first Malaysian to conduct winter research in Antarctica, spending seven months on the continent.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Gentari steps up push for 30-40 GW renewable capacity by 2030
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Global clean energy company Gentari is strengthening efforts to achieve its target of 30 to 40 gigawatts (GW) of renewable energy (RE) capacity by 2030, underpinned by rapid growth but contingent on market readiness and returns.
Gentari chief strategy and portfolio officer M Huzaini Ghazali said the company remained committed to the target, although delivery would depend on both execution capability and external conditions.
"We are still pushing. I would not completely guarantee it, but I would not say that we are not too far off," he told Bernama.
Huzaini noted that Gentari's capacity has expanded from about 0.4 GW to 9,100 to-date, both in installed and under construction.
He emphasised that this growth trajectory demonstrated Gentari's ability to scale, but cautioned that achieving the full target would also hinge on whether markets are able to provide the returns required to justify continued capital deployment.
"Whether or not we get there is not only dependent on our continued ability to do so. It is also determined by the market readiness in terms of giving us the returns that we require," said Huzaini.
Gentari's growth momentum has been supported by improving policy and market conditions, particularly as governments and corporations accelerate the shift towards cleaner energy amid rising conventional fuel costs.
"For countries like Malaysia, it is now the impetus for us to accelerate our growth in clean energy, from a government and policy perspective as well as an ambition of a nation," he said.
Huzaini said that rising hydrocarbon-based energy prices were prompting corporations to reassess their energy sourcing strategies, with renewables offering long-term cost certainty due to high upfront capital expenditure and relatively low operating costs, allowing pricing visibility over extended periods.
While Gentari continues to assess merger and acquisition opportunities, these would not be pursued solely to boost capacity, with priority on returns and portfolio fit.
The company currently operates across Malaysia, India, Australia and Taiwan, with the bulk of its installed capacity in India.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RM18bil flows into JS-SEZ as Johor doubles down on growth push
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Johor has stepped up its bid to anchor Southeast Asia's next growth corridor, with more than RM18 billion in foreign direct investments (FDI) already channelled into the Johor–Singapore Special Economic Zone (JS-SEZ) since 2024.
Menteri Besar Datuk Onn Hafiz Ghazi said the JS-SEZ had moved beyond concept to execution, drawing "real investments, real connectivity and real transformation" on the back of deepening integration with Singapore.
Speaking at the Johor–Singapore Special Economic Zone Strategic Forum 2026, co-organised by United Overseas Bank (UOB) and Invest Johor, he said growth momentum was being driven by policy clarity, public-private alignment and sustained investor engagement.
"Johor is no longer speaking in terms of potential. We are seeing momentum translated into tangible outcomes," he said.
UOB said it had facilitated over RM18 billion in FDI into the JS-SEZ via its Green Lane initiative with Invest Johor, alongside securing fresh commitments exceeding RM1 billion.
Johor's investment profile has strengthened in tandem, with a record RM110 billion in approved investments in 2025, the highest ever by any Malaysian state, while revenue rose to a historic RM2.67 billion.
Singapore remained Malaysia's top foreign investor at RM58.3 billion, reflecting the depth of the bilateral partnership underpinning the JS-SEZ.
Despite geopolitical headwinds, including the Middle East conflict and energy market volatility, Onn Hafiz noted continued confidence in Malaysia's fundamentals.
The International Monetary Fund has projected Malaysia's 2026 growth at 4.7 per cent, while Bank Negara Malaysia expects expansion of four to five per cent.
However, he cautioned against complacency, warning that inflationary pressures and supply chain disruptions could weigh on households and businesses.
"Periods of uncertainty demand stronger cooperation, not retreat. The JS-SEZ provides that platform," he said.
Johor is targeting gross domestic product growth of about 7 per cent, driven by high-impact sectors including advanced manufacturing, the digital economy, green energy and smart logistics.
The state has also intensified global outreach, including a recent mission to Shanghai, China, that drew more than 150 corporate leaders, with 36 indicating firm or strong investment intent.
Onn Hafiz said Johor would continue engaging investors to ensure inflows translate into jobs, stronger industries and long-term value creation.
"The JS-SEZ is a once-in-a-generation opportunity. It is real, taking shape and being executed as we speak," he said.
Meanwhile, UOB Malaysia chief executive officer Ng Wei Wei said the bank was focused on converting investor interest into execution by aligning Johor's priorities with Singapore's role as a global business hub.
"Cross-border alignment enables companies to move beyond intent and execute effectively, while strengthening regional supply chains," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Zahid denies claim of state polls delay over 'fear of defeat'
Article:
DUNGUN: Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi has dismissed claim by Bersatu president Tan Sri Muhyiddin Yassin that the unity government plans to delay state elections in Melaka and Johor out of fear of defeat.
Zahid, who is also Umno president, said the former prime minister should not assume the current administration would act in the same way as the government did under his leadership.
"Muhyiddin should not assume that others will do what he did before," he told reporters after officiating the opening of the Dungun Mara Junior Science College (MRSM) here today.
The Barisan Nasional chairman was responding to remarks by Muhyiddin, who reportedly suggested that any move to
postpone the elections
would be driven by concerns over waning public support.
The issue arose after Melaka DAP chairman Khoo Poay Tiong was reported to have proposed a delay to ease the burden on voters facing rising living costs linked to the global energy crisis.
Zahid stressed that there is no question of postponing the state polls in Melaka and Johor as the dissolution of state legislative assemblies rests with the respective rulers.
"The decision lies with the Governor (of Melaka) and the Regent (of Johor), as well as His Majesty the King of Malaysia, as the power to dissolve the assemblies rests with them," he added.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor-Singapore SEZ enters delivery phase as Johor tailors ecosystem for investors
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Johor is sharpening its pitch to global investors by repositioning the Johor-Singapore Special Economic Zone (JS-SEZ) as a fully integrated, demand-driven investment platform, with customised talent pipelines and shared infrastructure emerging as the centrepiece of its next growth phase.
State investment and economic adviser Datuk Seri Hasni Mohammad said the focus has shifted from broad promotion to execution, which aligns investor requirements on workforce, utilities and regulatory clarity to accelerate project realisation within the cross-border zone.
"We are no longer just attracting investors; we are configuring the ecosystem around them," he said, adding that Johor is tailoring labour and skills deployment through the Johor Talent Development Council (JTDC) to meet precise industry needs.
The recalibration comes as Malaysia seeks to capitalise on supply chain reconfiguration and deepen economic integration with Singapore, positioning the JS-SEZ as a complementary platform that blends Singapore's capital and expertise with Johor's land, scale and labour pool.
The JS-SEZ is entering a critical phase where delivery instead of policy signalling will determine its success.
Hasni said investor concerns are increasingly centred on operational certainty, particularly around utilities, talent availability and long-term sustainability commitments.
Recent requirements for data centre operators to develop alternative water sources are part of a broader push to embed resilience into large-scale investments.
"This is about future-proofing. Investors are expected to co-invest in infrastructure that ensures long-term viability," he said, adding that it was not a reflection of supply constraints.
The same principle applies to energy, with a growing emphasis on efficiency technologies as global competition for resources intensifies amid the ongoing Middle East crisis.
The JS-SEZ's value proposition lies in its ability to function as a seamless economic corridor rather than two separate jurisdictions, he said.
Efforts are underway to streamline processes and improve coordination among agencies to reduce friction for investors operating across borders.
Earlier, Malaysian Investment Development Authority officials highlighted that multi-stakeholder collaboration anchored on a holistic ecosystem approach would be key to
accelerating Johor's transformation.
Hasni said continuous engagement with investors, particularly those based in Singapore, is critical to addressing misconceptions and ensuring policy clarity.
"Direct communication matters. Misunderstandings can affect investment decisions if not addressed early," he said.
While the JS-SEZ is expected to draw investments in high-growth sectors such as advanced manufacturing, logistics and digital infrastructure, underlying risks remain.
Malaysia's reliance on imported inputs continues to expose it to supply disruptions, with heightened global competition for essential materials adding pressure.
Hasni said enforcement against smuggling and leakages must be strengthened to protect domestic supply, even as the state builds capacity to support large-scale industrial expansion.
Johor's port infrastructure remains a critical pillar of the JS-SEZ strategy, providing the throughput capacity needed to support rising trade volumes.
"Our ports are operating normally and are capable of handling both imports and exports efficiently," Hasni said.
As global firms rethink supply chains, the ability to offer reliable logistics alongside customised investment solutions is expected to determine whether Johor can convert interest into sustained capital inflows.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Work on Perak elephant sanctuary to kick off this year
Article:
IPOH: Construction of Phase One of the Perak Elephant Sanctuary in Hulu Perak is scheduled to begin this year, marking a key step in the state government's efforts to strengthen wildlife conservation and biodiversity management.
State Science, Environment and Green Technology Committee chairman Teh Kok Lim said the project, approved under the 13th Malaysia Plan, will be implemented in phases, with the first phase focusing on core infrastructure development.
"Phase One will involve the construction of an office complex, elephant enclosures and staff quarters, with completion targeted by the end of 2030.
"The total cost for this phase is estimated at RM52.13 million," he said during his winding-up speech at the state assembly sitting at Bangunan Perak Darul Ridzuan here today.
Teh said the sanctuary will be developed on a 79.74ha site at Batu 11 along the East–West Highway in Temenggor, Hulu Perak, an area chosen for its proximity to the natural habitat of wild elephants.
He said the land had been gazetted following approval by the state executive council, reflecting the state's commitment to wildlife conservation and sustainable, conservation-based ecotourism.
"Wildlife conservation efforts have often focused primarily on capture and translocation, with less emphasis on a landscape ecology-based approach.
"Through the development of the Perak Elephant Sanctuary, we aim to adopt a more holistic and sustainable model," he said.
Teh added that the project is expected to become a new landmark for Perak, serving as a centre for education, research and tourism, while raising public awareness of environmental protection.
He said Phase Two of the project will focus on the development of ecotourism facilities aimed at enhancing the visitor experience without compromising conservation priorities.
Meanwhile, he said preliminary works for Phase One, such as land surveys, utility mapping and soil investigations, have already commenced.
"These works are being undertaken by the Public Works Department through appointed consultants, with an allocation of RM500,000," he said.
"Special attention is being given to the project's impact on wildlife habitats, particularly in ensuring the sustainability of the elephant ecosystem and maintaining environmental balance," he said.
He added that the project is also expected to benefit local communities through socio-economic development initiatives and inclusive stakeholder engagement.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kedah MB urges assemblymen to safeguard integrity, fight corruption
Article:
ALOR STAR: Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Muhammad Sanusi Md Nor called on state assemblymen in Kedah to play a firmer role as guardians of integrity and a check-and-balance mechanism, to ensure governance remains transparent, accountable and free from corruption.
He said that in today's modern administrative landscape, the government can no longer operate in silos but requires an integrated approach involving all parties, including elected representatives and enforcement agencies.
"The role of an assemblyman is not merely to convey the people's aspirations, but to ensure every policy is understood, implemented effectively, and monitored transparently at the grassroots level," he said at the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) engagement programme with Kedah Assemblymen 2026, Wisma Darul Aman today.
He added that the implementation of the National Anti-Corruption Strategy (NACS) 2024–2028 is a timely step toward systematically combating corruption, covering key areas such as education, public accountability, the people's voice, enforcement, and incentives.
Success, he said, demands unwavering commitment from all parties to turn words into action, because the fight against corruption cannot be won with a mere policy document.
In light of this, he reminded that every government allocation must be managed with integrity, every project implemented transparently, and every decision made without conflict of interest.
"We cannot compromise on integrity. Once public trust is eroded, the impact is not just on the individual, but on the entire state administration," he emphasised.
He also described the MACC as a vital partner in strengthening the anti-corruption framework, particularly through educational approaches and more sustainable system improvements.
Meanwhile, at the same programme, MACC deputy chief commissioner (Prevention) Datuk Azmi Kamaruzaman said corruption does not stem from weak laws or regulations, but from systems that lack effective control and transparent oversight.
"When we look at the issue of leakage in public fund management, we must understand that it is not merely a financial issue.
"It reflects the entire governance chain, from planning right through to implementation, where every stage holds the potential to become a weak point if not properly controlled.
"When these weaknesses occur repeatedly, they are no longer isolated incidents. They become a pattern, which then forms a norm, and ultimately weakens the entire system," he explained.
— Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Foodcourt shut, area subdued after triple shooting
Article:
KOTA TINGGI: Shops were closed, so too were several business premises along Jalan Mangga Dua in Taman Kota Jaya yesterday which was the scene of a triple murder on Sunday afternoon.
One of the restaurants belonged to a victim who was gunned down by a 71-year-old man. Also killed were another man and a Vietnamese woman.
A hawker at a nearby food court, who declined to be named, said the victim's restaurant had no customers at the time of the shooting as the owner and workers were preparing to close for the day.
"The atmosphere was normal that afternoon. Business was as usual and the shop was about to close. There were no customers when the suspect arrived.
"It's fortunate the victim's child did not come yesterday. Usually, the child would be there. Otherwise, the child might have witnessed the shooting or even been harmed," the witness said.
She added that several neighbouring stalls did not open yesterday, noting that some premises in the area operate during the day while others open at night.
Recounting the incident, the woman in her 30s said she initially mistook the sound of gunfire for routine operations by local authorities shooting crows in the area.
Moments later, her child rushed into the shop and urged her to pull down the roller shutter.
"I was shocked. When I looked out, I saw the suspect holding a gun. Only then did I realise the shots were not meant for crows, but people," she said.
She claimed the suspect was known in the area and was a regular customer at the victim's shop.
The witness also recalled hearing a commotion prior to the shooting and understood that a scuffle had taken place before the suspect retrieved a firearm from his vehicle.
"After the shooting, I opened the shutter slightly to see what was happening. The suspect was still holding the gun and the victims were already covered in blood.
"He then left the scene. A trader nearby called the police. I later heard the suspect went to a nearby shop for a drink before he was arrested.
"Before that, he even told members of the public that he had shot people. We were all shocked and didn't know what to do except call the police. He was armed and we feared for our safety," she said.
In the 1.30pm incident on Sunday, three individuals were shot dead at the restaurant.
Johor police chief Ab Rahaman Arsad said the victims were aged between 37 and 63. All were pronounced dead at the scene from gunshot wounds from a shotgun.
The suspect a pig farm owner, was arrested about 30 minutes later, about 200m from the scene of the shooting.
Initial investigations indicate the shooting was triggered by a dispute over an unsettled loan between the suspect and one of the victims.
Police also confirmed that the firearm used, a Benelli M/Super shotgun  was licensed and registered to the suspect, who has no prior criminal record.
At the Kota Tinggi Magistrate's Court yesterday morning, the suspect was remanded for one week to facilitate investigations under Section 302 of the Penal Code for murder.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kota Tinggi shooting: Gunman was not drunk, cops say
Article:
KOTA TINGGI: The gunman in the fatal shooting that killed three people at a restaurant yesterday was not inebriated at the time, police said.
Police are now looking into the number of shots fired as investigations into the suspect's motive continue.
District police chief Superintendent Yusof Othman said the magistrate's court here has granted a seven-day remand order for the main suspect.
He confirmed today that the 71-year-old local man was not drunk at the time of the incident.
Earlier, there were claims that the suspect had lodged a police report after allegedly being assaulted by one of the victims during a debt collection dispute.
Johor police chief Datuk Ab Rahaman Arsad confirmed that one of the victims, a Vietnamese woman, owed money to the suspect.
However, he said the amount involved, previously reported as RM50,000, has yet to be confirmed.
"The debt issue is true, but the RM50,000 figure has not been confirmed," he said when contacted today.
Ab Rahaman also said a press conference would be held once investigations are completed and the investigation paper is referred to the deputy public prosecutor's (DPP) office.
"There will be a press conference once the investigation is completed and we receive instructions from the DPP," he said.
In the 1.30pm incident, the suspect — who holds a valid firearms licence — was arrested about 30 minutes later, roughly 200m from the scene.
Police also seized a Benelli M/Super shotgun believed to have been used in the shooting.
The suspect, a farmer with no prior criminal record, is
under remand
until Sunday to assist investigations under Section 302 of the Penal Code for murder.
The victims, aged between 37 and 63, died at the scene.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Jasin MP, Harakah apology to minister Fadhlina
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Jasin member of parliament Zulkifli Ismail and Harakah newspaper have issued a public apology to Education Minister Fadhlina Sidek over defamatory statements made in 2023.
Reading out the agreed judgment in the Sessions Court today, Zulkifli said the defendants acknowledged that the statements had led to misinterpretation and public misunderstanding, damaging the minister's reputation.
"We therefore agree to fully, unconditionally and unequivocally withdraw the statements," he said.
Judge Hilmiah Yusof recorded the consent judgment and ordered the three defendants to pay RM15,000 in costs to the plaintiff.
Fadhlina had filed the
defamation
suit against Zulkifli, former Harakah editor-in-chief Mazlan Jusoh and publisher Galeri Media Serbanika Sdn Bhd over allegations linked to restrictions on takbir recitations and the Qunut Nazilah prayer during a Palestine Solidarity Week.
- Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Mara mulls measures to support entrepreneurs affected by global economic situation - Zahid
Article:
DUNGUN: Mara is examining several support measures to assist entrepreneurs under its programmes who are affected by the global economic challenges due to the current geopolitical uncertainties.
Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi said among those being examined is to reduce the rental rate of premises for affected entrepreneurs.
Zahid, who is also the rural and regional development minister, said that apart from that, Mara will evaluate the implementation of rescheduling financing payments to give its entrepreneurs space to stabilise their financial position.
"At the same time, we have not forgotten the education aspect. Mara will continue to strengthen the implementation of the Budi Mara Education Programme (BUDI) to assist students from low-income families, including students from Maktab Rendah Sains Mara (MRSM), so that they can continue their studies without financial constraints.
"This proactive measure is important, because in any economic situation, we cannot compromise in ensuring access to quality education continues to be maintained.
"If an MRSM student is affected, the entire Mara ecosystem must rise to help him," he said when speaking at the opening ceremony of MRSM Dungun, here today.
When met by reporters after the ceremony, Zahid said that affected entrepreneurs were asked to contact and discuss with Mara to obtain assistance or a relaxation in the financing payments provided.
However, he said that his party would not compromise on entrepreneurs who were not affected but would take advantage of the relaxation provided.
"We are very concerned about the entrepreneurs who are financed or under the guidance of Mara. Not to mention small and medium-sized companies, we will find a better way," he said.
In another development, Zahid, who is also the chairman of the Central Disaster Management Committee, said the National Disaster Management Agency (Nadma) is currently coordinating assistance to victims of the fire in Kampung Bahagia, Batu Sapi, Sandakan, which occurred yesterday.
"Nadma has gone down to the ground to provide temporary evacuation sites… they (victims) are also registering now," he said.
More than
9,000 residents were affected
by the fire incident early yesterday morning but no casualties were reported.
Sandakan district police chief assistant commissioner George Abd Rakman said his department received a report on the incident at 1.30am before the location was declared a disaster area at 4am.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Terengganu Regent urges economic transformation to reduce dependence on Petroleum royalties
Article:
KUALA TERENGGANU: The Regent of Terengganu, Tengku Muhammad Ismail Sultan Mizan Zainal Abidin, today called on the state government to formulate strategies to shift from exporting raw materials to developing downstream products, in line with the Bumiputera Economic Transformation Plan 2035 launched by the federal government.
His Royal Highness said such structural economic transformation is crucial to reduce the state's dependence on volatile petroleum royalty income and to narrow development gaps between districts.
"By establishing a downstream industrial ecosystem, Terengganu will be able to strengthen its economic value chain through product development, job creation and more stable revenue generation.
"The development of downstream industrial clusters will also attract high-value investments and accelerate innovation-driven economic growth, positioning Terengganu as a sustainable and competitive state by 2030, in line with the Terengganu New Economy Transformation goals," he said when opening the fourth session of the 15th Terengganu State Legislative Assembly at Wisma Darul Iman here.
Also present was Tengku Sri Setia Mahkota Raja Tengku Dato Seri Muhammad Mua'az.
The Regent said imbalanced development between districts, such as Kemaman, which benefits from a more advanced industrial sector, compared with other districts, must be properly addressed to avoid serious socio-economic consequences.
Tengku Muhammad Ismail said agricultural production activities and infrastructure under the Terengganu Food Production Hub should continue to be strengthened to enable industry players to expand and increase output.
"The transformation of the agricultural sector must be implemented through the use of technology such as smart farming and precision agriculture, so that the sector evolves into a more modern and commercially viable industry. This effort will increase incomes and support the national food security agenda," he said.
In preparation for Visit Terengganu 2027, His Royal Highness said various tourism attractions such as cultural tourism, ecotourism and quality accommodation must be enhanced, while cleanliness should also be prioritised through efficient waste management.
"Therefore, I propose that the state government take the necessary steps to instil a culture of environmental cleanliness among the people," he said.
Tengku Muhammad Ismail also said non-radioactive rare earth elements have been identified as a new source of revenue with strong potential to be explored.
The Regent called on the government to establish proper planning and guidelines to ensure the resource is managed effectively and generates returns for the state.
"It is time for the state government to review its policy of exporting these minerals as raw materials and instead focus on downstream product development," he said.
His Royal Highness added that the East Coast Rail Link project in Terengganu has reached 97 per cent completion and is expected to transform the state's logistics landscape into an East Coast logistics hub.
In this regard, Tengku Muhammad Ismail urged the state government to leverage the infrastructure to enhance competitiveness within the domestic economy.
The Regent said housing indicators under the Malaysian Well-being Index showed a decline of 1.9 per cent in 2024 compared with 2023.
His Royal Highness said among the factors contributing to the decline were reduced home ownership affordability and the supply of housing units relative to B40 households.
"I hope efforts to provide good and comfortable housing facilities will be intensified to improve the quality of life of the people in the long term, especially for low-income groups," he said.
Meanwhile, Tengku Muhammad Ismail stressed that integrity must remain a key pillar in governance to maintain public trust.
"The application of ethical values must go beyond documentation and be embedded in transparent work practices at all levels of the organisation.
"I call for more initiatives to strengthen integrity in order to cultivate a high-integrity culture," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Major 7.5-magnitude quake hits off Japan, tsunami warning issued
Article:
TOKYO: An earthquake with a magnitude of 7.5 struck off the northeastern coast of Japan today, as authorities urged residents to stay away from coastal areas where tsunami waves of up to 3 metres (9.84 ft) were expected.
The tremor had an epicentre in the Pacific Ocean and was 10 km deep, according to the Japan Meteorological Agency.
The biggest waves were expected in Iwate, Aomori and Hokkaido prefectures, authorities said.
Speaking to reporters, Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi said the government had set up an emergency task force and urged citizens in the affected areas to evacuate to safety.
Broadcaster NHK showed ships sailing out of Hachinohe port in Hokkaido in anticipation of the waves, as an alert 'Tsunami! Evacuate!' flashed across the screen.
Bullet train services in Aomori at the northern tip of Japan's main Honshu island were halted due to the tremors, Kyodo news agency reported.
The quake measured an 'upper 5' on Japan's seismic intensity scale – strong enough to make it difficult for people to move around. In many cases, unreinforced concrete-block walls collapse.
Japan is one of the world's most earthquake-prone countries, with a tremor occurring at least every five minutes. Located in the "Ring of Fire" of volcanoes and oceanic trenches partly encircling the Pacific Basin, Japan accounts for about 20 per cent of the world's earthquakes of magnitude 6.0 or more.
There are no nuclear power plants currently in operation in Hokkaido and Tohoku regions but Hokkaido Electric Power Co and Tohoku Electric Power Co have a number of shutdown nuclear power plants there.
Tohoku Electric said it was checking the impact of the earthquake and tsunami on its Onagawa nuclear power plant. - REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Displaced Lebanese hesitant to return home as lasting truce uncertain
Article:
AFTER the ceasefire in Lebanon took hold, Samah Hajoul headed back to her apartment in south Beirut – but only long enough to grab fresh clothes, feeling safer in her tent as she wonders whether the truce will last.
"I am afraid to return to my home because the situation is not stable yet," the displaced mother of four told AFP from her campsite on the capital's seafront.
The ceasefire, which entered its second day Saturday, has granted many people the chance to check on their homes in areas where Hizbollah holds sway, like the southern suburbs, which were heavily bombed by Israel.
Hajoul found her apartment lightly damaged, with broken windows, but was only there to "bathe the children and get summer clothes" as temperatures started to rise.
"We do not feel safe to return, for fear that something might happen at night and I would not be able to carry my children and flee with them," she added.
Hajoul had to leave her neighbourhood when Hizbollah drew Lebanon into the Middle East conflict on March 2 with rocket attacks on Israel, prompting widespread Israeli airstrikes and a ground invasion in the country's south.
Like many other displaced residents, Hajoul was waiting to "see what happens" at the end of the 10-day truce before deciding whether to go back.
"If the ceasefire is consolidated, we will return to our homes," she said.
In the heavily bombed suburbs, neighbourhoods were still largely empty on Saturday, aside from those making quick visits to their homes, according to an AFP correspondent.
Among them was Hassan, 29, who only picked up a few things before returning to a school turned government shelter.
Hassan, who only gave his first name, pointed to "tension" surrounding Israeli strikes in the south and Iran's announcement it was again closing the Strait of Hormuz, rattling the ongoing ceasefire in the broader war.
With both Israel and Hizbollah accusing each other of breaching the truce in Lebanon, Hassan said "there is no indication that there is a solution."
[[nid:1422146]]
"We are afraid that if we return to the suburbs we will lose our place in the school to which we were displaced."
Israeli attacks on Lebanon killed nearly 2,300 people over the course of the war and displaced more than a million, according to Lebanese authorities, mostly from southern Lebanon and Beirut's southern suburbs.
On Saturday, senior Hizbollah official Mahmud Qamati warned that "Israeli treachery is expected at any time, and this is a temporary truce."
"Take a breath, relax a little, but do not abandon the places you have taken refuge in until we are completely reassured about your return" to your homes, he said.
AFP photographers saw heavy traffic heading south from Beirut in the morning, and similarly heavy traffic returning to the capital in the afternoon.
According to local media and residents, Israeli forces continue to carry out demolition and bombing operations on homes in several border villages.
The Israeli army on Saturday said it attacked "terrorists in several areas in southern Lebanon", noting that the military was authorised to take action against imminent threats in spite of the ceasefire.
Lebanese authorities hope the consolidation of the ceasefire will allow the displaced to return to their homes and compel Israeli troops to withdraw from the south.
The Israeli military said Saturday it had established a "Yellow Line" in southern Lebanon, similar to the one separating its forces from territory still held by Hamas in Gaza.
In the south, Lebanon's military and local bodies have been working to open roads that were blocked due to Israeli strikes since the early hours of the ceasefire.
In Hanawayh, east of the southern city of Tyre, deputy mayor Mustapha Bazzoun pledged to "restore life by securing all services, from communications to opening roads, so that people can return to their normal lives as quickly as possible."
"People are returning, but cautiously. We are working based on the idea that their return will be permanent. They may leave temporarily, but they will return later."
* The article is from AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sister of cop allegedly killed by drink-driver demands heavy sentence
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Naim Mullah Mohd Affandi had applied to join the police force three times before he was finally accepted.
He had been a parcel delivery rider in his village, torn about leaving his ailing family behind until his sister talked him into it.
"It was his dream to become a police officer and serve the community. So I encouraged him to accept the opportunity," said his sister, Nur Lylatul Qadrina.
He was 23 years old and barely a year into the job when a drink-driver struck him on Aug 18 last year in Temiang, Negri Sembilan.
He was serving at the Jalan Campbell police station in Seremban at the time.
Nur Lylatul and Naim was raised by their grandparents, Yusoff Mamat, 81, and Rahmah Hamad, 74, who worked as rubber tappers to put them through school, because their mother Rahian Yusoff, 48, could not work due to a condition requiring specialist monitoring.
Rahian is an only child and the three of them had lived with the grandparents since childhood.
Their grandparents, said Nur Lylatul, feel his absence so acutely that they cannot bring themselves to look at the clothes he left behind.
"My grandparents sacrificed for us, including putting us through education. The loss of my brother is felt most deeply by them," she said.
Even while posted far from home, Naim had called the family every single day without fail.
"Even when there was nothing particular to talk about, he would still call just to ask how we were," his sister said.
On Aug 22, K. Kelvin, 27, was
charged in the magistrate's court
with driving under the influence of alcohol, resulting in Naim's death.
Kelvin was charged under Section 44(1)(b) of the Road Transport Act and face imprisonment of not less than 10 years and not more than 15 years, a fine between RM50,000 and RM100,000, and disqualification from holding a driving licence for at least 10 years from the date of conviction.
But Nur Lylatul said the accused is currently free on bail, a situation she found deeply unjust.
"The bail granted is insufficient because he may well be able to afford it.
"We hope a heavier sentence is imposed on that man, to serve as a lesson to him and to others," she said.
On the question of monetary compensation or diyat, she was unequivocal: no sum of money could substitute for justice.
"Even if the compensation runs into hundreds of thousands of ringgit, it cannot bring my brother back.
"Monetary compensation is wholly disproportionate unless a heavy sentence is also imposed on the perpetrator," she said.
Asked about Transport Minister Anthony Loke's statement that the death penalty need not be introduced under the Road Transport Act, given that the mandatory death penalty had already been abolished and that drivers could be charged under the Penal Code, Nur Lylatul Qadrina said her family's position was simple.
"We only hope that a heavy sentence is imposed on the perpetrator, not just a short jail term.
"The punishment must be proportionate to what we endure and feel. If there is a death penalty, we support it," she said, adding that the government should conduct a thorough study and ensure that all considerations, including the trauma borne by families, were taken into account.
She also backed proposals to extend legal liability to alcohol sellers and premises operators who fail to stop visibly intoxicated customers from driving.
"The source of the problem is there. The alcohol sellers. Because sellers face no consequences, they are free to sell alcohol to anyone.
"After that, those who have consumed alcohol drive on public roads while intoxicated. The lives of other innocent people will be sacrificed," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fahmi: Second Malaysian oil tanker expected to arrive this weekend
Article:
MUAR: The second Malaysian oil-laden vessel that passed through the Strait of Hormuz is expected to arrive in the country by the end of the week, Communications Minister Datuk Fahmi Fadzil said.
He said the shipment, following the arrival of the first vessel on April 17 carrying one million barrels of crude oil, would help strengthen Malaysia's supply security.
"This will ensure sufficient crude oil stocks for processing at local refineries, allowing supply, particularly for Petronas, to remain adequate through to the end of June," he said after checking the implementation of the Budi Madani RON95 (Budi95) and diesel subsidy system at petrol stations in Pagoh today.
Also present was Johor Domestic Trade and Cost of Living director Lilis Saslinda Pornomo.
Fahmi, who is also the Madani government spokesman, reiterated that the temporary adjustment of the Budi95 subsidy quota to 200 litres per month would remain in place until national fuel supply stabilises.
While acknowledging calls, including from media practitioners, for the quota to be restored, Fahmi said fuel supply issues are expected to be discussed at the National Economic Action Council meeting tomorrow.
It was reported that the f
irst of the seven vessels arrived
at the Pengerang Terminal in Johor on April 17.
Chartered by Petco, a unit of Malaysian state energy firm Petroliam Nasional Bhd (Petronas), the vessel carried about one million barrels of Iraqi Basrah heavy crude.
Meanwhile, Fahmi said fuel supply across 639 petrol stations in Johor remains stable, with no disruptions or shortages reported.
During his two-day working visit to the state, several initiatives were launched, including collaboration with telecommunications companies to improve internet coverage along 1,700km of Keretapi Tanah Melayu Bhd (KTMB) rail lines.
Fahmi also launched a Safe Internet Campaign at Tun Syed Nasir Syed Ismail Polytechnic in Pagoh, aimed at equipping youth to navigate digital risks, and presented appointment letters to 150 Madani community leaders to strengthen grassroots communication of government policies.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: False promise leads to life on the streets
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Amid the bustle of the capital, stories of hardship among the homeless continue to unfold, with many forced to seek shelter along five-foot ways and public spaces.
For some, it is not a choice but the result of misfortune, including job loss and deception.
Worse still, without family or acquaintances to rely on, they depend solely on public generosity and the kindness of strangers.
A man who wished to be known only as Nazri, 30, said a false promise from a friend led to his downfall, leaving him stranded and homeless in the capital after allegedly being cheated out of his money.
"I was offered help by a friend who promised me a job. I paid RM800, but when I arrived in Kuala Lumpur, I realised I had been scammed. All contact numbers were blocked.
"I saved that money while working as a bus ticket seller in Larkin, Johor. I waited for a week but received no response.
"Now I have only RM16 left, not even enough for the fare home," he said.
He said he has been living on the streets for a week around Pasar Seni, with no friends or family in Kuala Lumpur.
He now depends entirely on public assistance for food and survival.
"I don't know how long I can live like this. I came to the city hoping to change my family's fate, but it turned out this way instead," he said.
Meanwhile, Siva, in his 40s from Klang, said his life took a downturn after losing his job in the post-Covid-19 period.
He said he previously worked in a factory but now struggles to secure stable employment.
"I sleep wherever I can. I used to work in a factory.
"I came to Kuala Lumpur with help from a friend who gave me RM5 for transport. Sometimes I only return to Klang once a week," he said.
Despite the hardship, Siva said he continues to live alongside others in similar circumstances.
"People here don't judge us.
"We spend time together and make the most of what we have," he said.
However, he admitted that some are less welcoming to newcomers, and issues involving intoxicated individuals do occur, though the situation remains under control.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: SC, Bursa roll out MY Value Up to drive value creation among PLCs
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Securities Commission (SC) and Bursa Malaysia Bhd today launched the MY Value Up Programme to boost the valuation and market appeal of Malaysian public-listed companies (PLCs).
Aligned with the Capital Market Masterplan 2026-2030, the initiative is a joint commitment by the SC and Bursa Malaysia to foster a more dynamic corporate landscape.
The MY Value Up Programme encourages companies to articulate and disclose their mid- to long-term strategies, helping PLCs signal stronger corporate ambition and deepen engagement with domestic and regional investors.
In a joint statement, the regulators said this would lead to more transparent and forward-looking disclosures, enhancing investor confidence and supporting more informed capital allocation decisions.
SC chairman Datuk Mohammad Faiz Azmi said the new masterplan sets a bold vision for a more dynamic and competitive capital market.
He said the commission aims to shift from a culture of compliance to one focused on active value creation.
"It is an opportune time for our leading PLCs to widen their lens and benchmark themselves against the best in class, not just domestically but also regionally and beyond," he said.
Earlier today, the SC held an engagement session with chairpersons and C-suite executives of the top 88 PLCs on Bursa Malaysia, along with capital market industry leaders.
These companies collectively account for 80 per cent of Bursa Malaysia's total market capitalisation, with each having a minimum market capitalisation of about RM4 billion.
Bursa Malaysia chief executive officer Datuk Fad'l Mohamed said the programme is designed to raise the visibility and market recognition of underlying corporate performance among participating PLCs.
He said the initiative aims to strengthen market signals through clearer strategy articulation, stronger performance discipline and deeper investor engagement.
"Ultimately, this supports more effective price discovery and contributes to a more vibrant capital market that recognises and rewards long-term corporate value," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man shot in face during attempted cattle theft in Felda Kemahang 1
Article:
TANAH MERAH: A settler's son was injured after he was shot by a group of men believed to be cattle smugglers in Felda Kemahang 1 today.
In the 3am incident, the victim, in his 40s, was sleeping when a group of four to five men entered the village and attempted to steal cattle.
Sources said the victim realised what was happening and shouted to alert villagers, who rushed out of their homes and tried to surround the suspects.
"The suspects fired several shots, one of which struck the victim on the cheek."
The victim was rushed to the district hospital and is reported to be in stable condition.
NST has contacted the police for comment.
Another source claimed the group is believed to be part of an active cattle smuggling syndicate operating along the Malaysia-Thailand border.
They are believed to be working in cahoots with locals to steal livestock for resale.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Perak to form special committee to tackle telecom cable theft, sabotage
Article:
IPOH: Perak will establish a special cross-agency committee to coordinate enforcement and response efforts against trespassing, sabotage and theft involving telecommunications infrastructure, including cable theft.
State Communications, Multimedia and Non-Governmental Organisations (NGO) Committee chairman Mohd Azlan Helmi said the committee would be chaired by him, with the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) as secretariat, while telecommunications companies and enforcement agencies, including the police, would also be involved.
He said acts of vandalism, including tampering with manholes to steal cables or disrupting telecommunications facilities, were serious offences and could be prosecuted under Section 325 of the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998, which carries a fine of up to RM500,000, imprisonment of up to five years, or both.
Azlan said the provision was expected to be a stronger deterrent, as many cable theft cases had previously been prosecuted under general theft offences carrying lighter penalties.
He said the committee would hold its first meeting at the end of this month.
Earlier, during the winding-up session of the royal address at the State Legislative Assembly sitting, Azlan said open manhole cases linked to cable theft had worsened since 2024, posing risks to road users when covers were not replaced.
He said 539 cases were recorded that year, rising sharply to 908 in 2025.
"As of March this year, 377 cases have been recorded, an increase of 68 per cent compared with the same period last year," he said.
He said the trend showed that trespassing and open manhole incidents remained widespread despite various measures, underscoring the need for a more comprehensive approach involving agencies and local communities.
Meanwhile, Azlan said a Rapid Response Team (RRT) had also been set up with MCMC and Telekom Malaysia (TM) to strengthen public safety, particularly in handling open manholes.
He said 14 RRT teams had been deployed statewide since April 15, with three based in Kinta and one each in Kampar, Perak Tengah, Batang Padang, Hilir Perak, Hulu Perak, Kerian, Kuala Kangsar, Larut Matang dan Selama, Manjung, Muallim and Bagan Datuk.
"The main function of the RRT is to respond immediately to complaints or reports of open manholes. Immediate action includes closing the manhole or placing safety cones or barricades as temporary protection," he said.
He added that WhatsApp groups involving local councils, police, elected representatives and village heads had also been formed to improve communication and speed up information sharing on telecom infrastructure issues.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Singapore GasCo secures extra LNG cargoes, eyes term deals despite war volatility
Article:
SINGAPORE: Singapore's state gas buyer GasCo has secured additional cargoes of liquefied natural gas to offset supply shortfalls triggered by the US-Israel war on Iran, and plans to seek long-term supply deals this year, despite market volatility.
The city-state, which relies on gas to generate 95 per cent of its electricity has been importing increasing volumes of liquefied natural gas (LNG), reaching 5.93 million tonnes last year, Kpler data shows, nearly half of it shipped from Qatar.
"While a portion of our LNG imports has been curtailed, Singapore GasCo has taken proactive steps to secure additional cargoes to augment existing supply sources," Chief Executive Alan Heng told Reuters in an emailed response.
"In the short term, our focus is on securing LNG cargoes to make up for supply curtailed by existing suppliers."
TWO SPOT CARGOES FROM AUSTRALIA, ONE FROM MOZAMBIQUE
Kpler shiptracking data showed that Singapore received two spot cargoes from Australia and one from Mozambique since the war began on Feb 28.
One Australian cargo was delivered on March 27 from the APLNG project, and the other on April 9, from the Gorgon LNG project. The third spot cargo was delivered on April 19, after being loaded from the Coral South floating LNG project offshore Mozambique.
Singapore imported three LNG cargoes from Qatar in March last year, as part of full-year imports of 2.76 million tonnes from the Gulf supplier.
GasCo is unable to comment on specific commercial arrangements, it told Reuters in reply to a request for comment on the spot cargo procurements and deliveries to Singapore.
STILL PLANS TO GO TO MARKET IN 2026 FOR LONG-TERM SUPPLIES
On its term-deal LNG procurement plans, GasCo still plans to go to the market in 2026 to seek long-term supplies, said Heng.
"At this time, the market has been disrupted and remains highly volatile," he added. "Engagements with credible, trusted suppliers are ongoing."
In November, Heng had said GasCo would seek offers in the first quarter of this year for delivery of term LNG supply from 2028.
Since the beginning of the war, Iran's blockage of the Strait of Hormuz, which handles 20 per cent of global LNG flows, and damage to Qatar's liquefaction trains, boosted Asian LNG prices to three-year highs, before paring some gains.
They were last up 54 per cent since end-February at US$16.05 per million British thermal units.
ANALYSTS SLASH GLOBAL SUPPLY OUTLOOKS
Analysts have also slashed global supply outlooks, and expect high prices and the supply shortage to lead to demand destruction across Asia.
"The situation remains fluid, and we expect continued uncertainty and volatility in the market," said Heng.
The company aimed to work closely with regulators, gas suppliers, and power generation companies to safeguard the reliability of Singapore's energy supply, he added.
"We are actively managing and monitoring supply curtailments to Singapore and procuring additional LNG supply where necessary."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Thunderstorms in KL, Putrajaya and eight states until 6pm - MetMalaysia
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Thunderstorms, heavy rain and strong winds are forecasted to hit Kuala Lumpur, Putrajaya, and eight states until 6pm today, the Malaysian Meteorological Department (MetMalaysia) said.
The eight affected states are Perak (Larut, Matang and Selama, Kuala Kangsar, Kinta, Kampar, Batang Padang and Muallim),  Pahang (Cameron Highlands, Lipis, Raub, Bentong, Bera and Rompin), Selangor (Hulu Selangor, Klang, Gombak, Petaling, Kuala Langat, Hulu Langat and Sepang), Negeri Sembilan (Jelebu, Seremban, Port Dickson, Jempol and Tampin), Melaka (Melaka Tengah and Jasin), Johor (Tangkak, Segamat, Muar, Batu Pahat and Pontian) and Sarawak (Kuching and Serian).
In Sabah, the affected areas include the Interior (Kuala Penyu and Beaufort), the West Coast (Papar, Putatan, Penampang, Kota Kinabalu, Tuaran and Kota Belud) and Kudat (Kota Marudu). – BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Michigan refuses Trump administration demand for 2024 election ballots
Article:
NEW YORK: Michigan officials on Sunday pushed back on a US Department of Justice demand for Detroit-area ballots and other materials related to the 2024 election, accusing the Trump administration of trying to cast doubt on the integrity of US elections.
The Justice Department last week sent a letter demanding ballots, ballot receipts and ballot envelopes to the clerk in Wayne County, home to the heavily Democratic-leaning city of Detroit, according to Michigan Attorney General Dana Nessel.
Nessel's office released the DOJ's letter, authored by Assistant Attorney General Harmeet Dhillon, along with a reply vowing to fight the request.
"This request is as absurd as it is baseless," Nessel said in a joint statement with Michigan Governor Gretchen Whitmer and Secretary of State Jocelyn Benson.
"If this administration wants to bring this circus to our state, my office is prepared to protect the people's right to vote."
Nessel, Whitmer and Benson are Democrats. President Donald Trump, a Republican, has long pushed the false claim that his 2020 election defeat to Democratic President Joe Biden was the result of widespread voter fraud.
Dhillon's letter focuses on 2024's elections, arguing they too need scrutiny.
But the 2020 election remains a prominent concern for many Trump administration officials.
In an interview with Fox News' "Sunday Morning Futures" programme, FBI Director Kash Patel pledged that arrests over alleged 2020 election issues are "coming soon."
On the same TV show, Dhillon touted the administration's efforts to get states to provide access to voter registration lists, saying the department has sued 29 states and the District of Columbia over their refusal of access to voter rolls.
DOJ staffers so far have reviewed 60 million voter records and found they included the names of 350,000 dead persons, said Dhillon, who leads the Justice Department's civil rights division.
She did not provide any evidence that votes were cast for those names.
In addition, about 25,000 people who lacked proof of citizenship were referred to the Department of Homeland Security "to dig into that further and see the extent to which people voted," she said.
The Justice Department has suffered multiple legal setbacks in its pursuit of election-related records, with judges ruling against requests in Rhode Island, California, Massachusetts, Michigan and Oregon.
A federal judge on Friday rejected the Justice Department's bid to force Rhode Island to turn over non-public data on nearly 750,000 registered voters so the Trump administration could probe "election integrity" in the Democratic-led state.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UMT student who drowned attempting to save friend was dean's award winner
Article:
BATU PAHAT: Suraya Hassan, the Universiti Malaysia Terengganu student who drowned attempting to save her friend yesterday, was a top scorer who consistently received the dean's award.
On Friday, she was up on stage receiving the award for the fifth consecutive time, choosing to wear a white baju kurung.
It was this choice of attire that concerned her 61-year-old mother, Rohana Mat Rejab.
"My daughter was determined to wear a white baju kurung when receiving her dean's award. I found it unusual, and it turned out to be a sign that she was about to leave us forever," said the distraught woman.
Suraya, an Applied Science (Nanophysics) student, was found drowned in Sungai Lata Ulu Kasar in Setiu, Terengganu, at noon yesterday.
The 22-year-old was there with a group of fellow UMT students and when one got into difficulties in the water, she and a friend had attempted to save the girl.
While the others were saved Suraya was swept away, her body found sometime later.
Rohana said when Suraya, the 11th of her 12 children, expressed her intention to wear the white outfit, she had suggested that her daughter reconsider and choose a different colour.
"When I said that, she still chose to wear it. I was quite surprised, but I tried to stay strong and not think negatively," she said when met at her home in Parit Haji Noor, Parit Imam, here today.
She added that on the morning of the drowning, she felt unusually restless, as though something was amiss.
"Nothing felt right… I felt directionless. Fear also overwhelmed me, and in my heart I kept asking what was going to happen to me.
"I couldn't even eat because of the fear. While I was resting, another of my children came to me in tears and told me Suraya was gone," she said, struggling to hold back her emotions.
Meanwhile, the victim's elder sister, Siti Mariam, 25, said Suraya had consistently received the dean's award every semester.
"She had never worn a white baju kurung to receive the award before. I knew she had two — one with floral patterns and another plain white.
"I was surprised, but at the time I didn't think much of it," she said.
She added that based on accounts of what happened, a friend had tried to extend a stick towards her sister when she had gotten into trouble in the water, but was unable to reach her as she had already been swept away by the current.
"What we know is that it started with an attempt to retrieve a ball. When her friend nearly drowned, she tried to help.
"One of her friends contacted me saying she had drowned, but at that moment it didn't hit me that had died. About 15 minutes later, another call confirmed that she was gone.
"Before the incident, I had contacted her and she said she was going out to relieve stress. It never crossed my mind that she was at the river," she said.
Suraya's remains are expected to be laid to rest at the Muslim cemetery in Parit Haji Salleh, Jalan Kluang, today.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Batu Pahat fatal crash: 'I stopped receiving their location updates,' says grieving mother
Article:
BATU PAHAT:  Yesterday, Norashikin Mohamad, 57, who was tailing her sons in a separate car, suddenly stopped receiving their live location updates. Her texts went unanswered.
She only found out later that they had been killed in a crash while on their way back to their polytechnic in Pasir Gudang.
The crash, which occurred at 4.30pm along Jalan Kluang in Ayer Hitam, claimed the lives of Fazli Yuzaini Zainal Abidin, 30, and Irfan Helmi, 20.
Their two other brothers — Azrul Nizam, 29, and Zul Fahmi, 26 — who were travelling with them, survived.
Azrul, who was driving, and Zul Fahmi were seriously injured and are being treated at Enche' Besar Hajjah Khalsom Hospital.
The family had been travelling from their home in Taman Mengkibol, Kluang, to send Irfan Helmi back to Politeknik Ibrahim Sultan, where he was a fourth-semester mechanical engineering diploma student.
[[nid:1422098]]
"We left earlier as they wanted to refuel. When we were about to reach Skudai, Azrul's friend called and asked me to check a video.
"I asked why, and he said Azrul's car had been involved in an accident. I immediately checked Facebook, but could not see clearly as the images only showed traffic congestion.
"Without wasting time, we turned back, but the road was heavily congested. It was during that journey that I was told that Fazli Yuzaini and Irfan Helmi had died," she said when met at the Sultanah Nora Ismail Hospital's Forensic Unit today.
She added that she had tried calling one of her sons for confirmation, but the call was answered by medical personnel.
"They only told me that two of my sons, Azrul Nizam and Zul Fahmi, were being treated in the yellow zone.
"Both suffered injuries to their backs and necks due to the impact," she said.
Their uncle, Zulkifli Mohamad, 55, said he rushed back from Penang to Johor upon receiving news of the crash.
"At that time, we did not know they had died. It was only around 6pm that I was told the actual situation.
"We are still in shock, but we have to accept it, even though the loss is deeply felt," he said.
The two siblings were laid to rest at the Jalan Padang Tembak cemetery in Kluang this afternoon.
District police chief Assistant Commissioner Shahrulanuar Mushaddat Abdullah Sani said the accident occurred during heavy rain, causing them to lose control of their car.
"The
vehicle veered into the opposite lane before colliding
with a sport utility vehicle (SUV) and another car.
"The two siblings died at the scene, while the SUV driver and the other motorist, aged 42 and 35 respectively, sustained minor injuries.
"The case is being investigated under Section 41(1) of the Road Transport Act," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Widow of drink-driving victim wants offender to provide for family for life
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: "I want the offender to provide for my family for life," said the widow of a man killed in a drink-driving crash, as she called for harsher penalties against such offences.
Nor Nadia Abdul Majid said she could not accept what she described as lenient punishment for those responsible for deaths caused by driving under the influence of alcohol.
Her husband, Amirul Hafiz Omar, 33, was killed when his motorcycle was hit by a car driven by a man alleged to have been under the influence of alcohol in Klang, Selangor on March 29.
He left behind Nor Nadia and three children, including Muhammad Aliff Izz Rayqal, 7, who still struggles to understand his father's death.
"Sometimes he is okay, sometimes he misses his father and asks all sorts of questions. 'Has Papa not called?', 'How do I call Papa? Do I have to die first before I can meet Papa?'" she said.
Nor Nadia said the incident had left her family's life in turmoil, forcing them to move to her mother-in-law's home in Kampung Repoh, Batu Kurau in Larut, Perak.
Amirul had been working two jobs — as a store assistant by day and a food delivery rider by night — earning between RM4,000 and RM5,000 a month.
Nor Nadia is now receiving a Perkeso survivor's pension of RM2,888 a month and plans to resume work as an e-commerce affiliate marketing agent.
On the legal front, she said she was concerned about the prosecution's direction in the case.
The accused had been charged under Section 302 of the Penal Code for murder, which carries the death penalty or imprisonment of between 30 and 40 years and whipping upon conviction.
However, she feared the charge may not be sustained and said she would not accept a lesser charge under the Road Transport Act 1987.
"In my view, the punishment under that provision is far too light," she said.
Nor Nadia also said she initially reacted with anger to Transport Minister Anthony Loke's remarks that the death penalty need not be introduced for road traffic offences, but later accepted it if replaced with a minimum of 20 years' imprisonment or life imprisonment with caning.
She said she intended to file a civil suit for RM1 million, although she acknowledged the legal process could take years.
She also called for a formal diyat mechanism under Islamic law to support families in similar situations.
She said that she wanted the offender to be responsible for her family's welfare if capital punishment was not imposed.
She also supported proposals to hold alcohol-serving premises accountable if patrons involved in accidents had been served alcohol.
"In my view, intoxication from alcohol is the root of all evils.
"So yes, I agree that premises owners should also be charged, because they failed to fulfil their obligations," she said.
On April 1,
R. Saktygaanapathy, 28, was charged
in the magistrate's court with Amirul's murder.
According to the facts of the case, he allegedly crashed his car head-on into Amirul, who was riding a motorcycle to buy a Mathematics book for his children.
Amirul was killed instantly after being struck by the vehicle, which was allegedly travelling at high speed towards Klang town from Jalan Raya Barat.
The accused is believed to have overtaken several vehicles before veering into the opposite lane and colliding head-on with the motorcycle.
The impact flung Amirul onto a Perodua Myvi. He was pronounced dead at the scene.
He was later laid to rest at the Kampung Repoh Islamic Cemetery in Larut, Perak.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Rumen Radev, ex-pilot who wants to give Bulgaria wings
Article:
FORMER fighter pilot Rumen Radev has vowed to lead Bulgaria out of a chronic political crisis, with his grouping winning Sunday's general election – the eighth in five years.
Many voters see Radev – who was Bulgaria's president until earlier this year – as the only person capable of giving the corruption-plagued Balkan nation a fresh start.
The 62-year-old has presented himself as a defender of the poor of the EU's poorest country as he walks a tightrope on European issues.
He has hailed the benefits Bulgaria has reaped from EU membership while calling for dialogue with Russia as its invasion of Ukraine rages into a fifth year of fighting.
"Bulgaria is in a unique position, because we are the only EU member state that is both Slavic and Eastern Orthodox. That should be used," Radev, who was president for nine years, said recently.
"And we really can be a very important link in this whole process, which I am sure will sooner or later begin, to restore relations with Russia," Radev added.
Last year he called for a referendum on Bulgaria entering the eurozone, saying it was not ready to join.
But he failed and Bulgaria adopted the single European currency on Jan 1.
Radev has also slammed military aid to Ukraine and the EU trying to turn its back on Russian oil and gas.
"Geographically, economically, in terms of resources and as a market, we need to rebuild those relations," he insisted.
For sociologist Parvan Simeonov, Radev is hard to figure out like many leaders in the region who, "depending on the visiting delegation, choose whether or not to fly the Ukrainian flag in the background."
Radev insists he embodies distrust of the country's elites and oligarchs, denying any links to them.
A graduate of the elite US Air War College, he later served as the head of the Bulgarian Air Force.
But he moved into politics in 2016 and later won a presidential election to the mostly ceremonial post.
Born on June 18, 1963 in the southeastern Soviet-era new town of Dimitrovgrad, the austere and reserved man does not have the polish of seasoned communicators.
When he vows to regulate public tenders through artificial intelligence or to reform the much?criticised judicial system, he sometimes gives the impression of reciting a memorised text.
But he won over many liberal pro-European voters when he openly supported protesters at 2020 rallies against corruption.
Radev walked out of the presidential palace with his fist raised to join the protests that ultimately toppled conservative prime minister Boyko Borissov a year later.
Radev was re-elected head of state in 2021 with two-thirds of the vote.
Late last year he once again backed anti-corruption protesters and when the last government resigned in December, he stepped down as president to run as premier.
Radev's centre-left movement, Progressive Bulgaria, brings together a plethora of figures including military officers, former socialist officials and athletes, and the union leader of the country's main arms manufacturer, which has boomed from supplying Ukraine's army.
Radev campaigns on combatting social inequalities and promoting budgetary discipline without calling for radical change, said Simeonov.
His promises of a return to stability appeal to voters tired of facing election after election.
Married with two children and intensely patriotic, Radev also woos voters with a modest lifestyle and his defence of what he calls family values.
A campaign video shot in a village shop that went viral showed Radev soothing the grocer upset with rising prices and Bulgaria's eurozone entry.
* The writer is from AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Azalina urges caution on Federal Court appointments ahead of Istana Negara ceremony
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Law and Institutional Reform) Datuk Seri Azalina Othman Said has advised the public to wait for official confirmation on the appointment of two Federal Court judges at a ceremony in Istana Negara tomorrow.
"It is better to wait until the ceremony is completed," she told reporters today.
Her remarks come amid reports that Court of Appeal judges Datuk Ravinthran Paramaguru and Datuk Azmi Ariffin are set to be elevated to the Federal Court.
Such appointments would fill all 11 positions at Malaysia's highest court.
[[nid:1422089]]
Ravinthran was appointed judicial commissioner in 2009 before becoming a High Court judge in 2013 and later a Court of Appeal judge in 2019.
Azmi was appointed judicial commissioner in 2014, elevated to the High Court in 2017, and promoted to the Court of Appeal in June 2023.
earlier , it was reported that six High Court judges – Dean Wayne Daly, Datuk Aliza Sulaiman, Datuk Amarjeet Singh Serjit Singh, Datuk Seri Mohd Radzi Harun, Datuk Seri Latifah Tahar and Datuk Ahmad Shahrir Mohd Salleh – are slated to be elevated to the Court of Appeal.
Meanwhile, judicial commissioners Dr Suzana Muhammad Said, Yusrin Faidz Yusoff, Arziah Mohamed Apandi, Gan Techiong, Datuk Hazizah Kassim and Suriyati Hasimah Mohd Hashim, appointed in 2023, are expected to be elevated as High Court judges.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Atomic Energy department finds no radiation compliance issues after investigation into UMMC’s alleged ‘cancer cluster’
Article:
DENGKIL, 20 April 2026 – The Ministry of Science, Technology and Innovation (MOSTI), through the Atomic Energy Department, has responded to claims of a cancer cluster at the Nuclear Medicine Unit of the University Malaya Medical Centre (PPUM).
In a statement, the department has confirmed that the Medical Radiation Regulatory Division (BKRP) of the Ministry of Health conducted an investigation into the alleged cancer cluster at PPUM.
The investigation found no critical radiation safety compliance issues that could be linked to the alleged cancer cluster at the unit.
Under the Atomic Energy Act 1984 (Act 304) and the Atomic Energy Licensing (Amendment) Act 2025 (A1779), the licensing authority for radioactive materials, nuclear materials and radiation generators for non-medical purposes is the Director General of the Atomic Energy Department (Atom Malaysia). For medical purposes, the licensing authority is the Director General of Health, Ministry of Health Malaysia (KKM).
Atom Malaysia and BKRP remain committed to ensuring the safety of radiation workers and the public.
Strict action as provided under Act 304 will be taken against any individual or licensee who fails to comply with Act 304, its regulations and licence conditions.

Title: Prosecution seeks to drop the word ‘received’ from Lim Guan Eng’s undersea tunnel corruption charge
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 20 — The prosecution has applied to amend the first charge against former Penang chief minister Lim Guan Eng in the RM6.3 billion major roads and undersea tunnel corruption case.
Deputy public prosecutor Datuk Wan Shaharuddin Wan Ladin informed Sessions Court Judge Azura Alwi that the amendment involves dropping the word “received” from the charge.
“The law under Section 23 of the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) Act 2009 does not specify whether a person receives or solicits a gratification; it merely requires that the gratification be for a purpose. The amount of RM3.3 million remains in the charge,” he said.
Lim’s lawyer, Ramkarpal Singh, objected to the application and stressed that the element of receipt is essential to the charge.
“If the word is removed without any explanation, and at such a late stage, six years later, it will prejudice the defence. That is why we need time to prepare our arguments,” he submitted.
Judge Azura then set tomorrow for both parties to present their arguments on the matter.
The trial then continued with the defence cross-examining the 37th prosecution witness, businessman Datuk Seri G Gnanaraja, 44.
Under the first charge, Lim, 65, is accused of abusing his position as Penang chief minister to receive RM3.3 million in return for helping a company owned by businessman Zarul Ahmad secure the Penang Major Roads and Tunnel Construction Project, valued at approximately RM6.34 billion.
The alleged offence took place at the Penang Chief Minister’s Office between January 2011 and August 2017.
The second charge states that the Bagan MP solicited a 10 per cent cut out of the project’s future profits from Zarul Ahmad for assisting his company in securing the same project.
The alleged offence occurred near The Gardens Hotel, Lingkaran Syed Putra, Mid Valley City, Kuala Lumpur, between 12.30 am and 2.00 am in March 2011.
